summary: The Fluency Fur podcast is back with a special guest. This week's guest is Carol, a family friend. Carol, thank you for joining us.
"So how are you?  I'm good.  Thank you.  So how is it going?" he asked. "I'm good," he replied.

I've been away for a few days in Worcestershire in the Malvern. I spent some time with a friend and I went to Stratford-on-Avon Shakespeare's  birthplace to see not a Shakespeare play, but a modern play, which is funny. I
It's a tiny country in terms of comparing it with Britain, about the  size of Wales. It's only about 200 miles at the greatest length.
The capital, Lublyana, there's no lid. It's very wooded, mountainous, green, green in two ways. So it's green because there are loads of pine tree, countryside.
Carol got to know Rachel's family through her parents. She recalls coming to one of Rachel's first birthday parties. She also remembers Wendy's effortless hospitality.
"I got to know Ian Moore because we ran quite a lot of training. And then he offered me some work. When I wanted to leave my day, he offered us six weeks work"
"I decided, right, I'm going to go.  Wow.  And that was my jumping off point," he says.
"We talked about deep personal stuff and we shared all our ups and downs," she says. "We used a book that was written by some experts"
David and Emily Grant spent many holidays together in France. David was the celebrant at Emily's wedding. Emily says she was honoured to be asked to do the ceremony.

The author has travelled extensively, but is there a place that changed her instantly? She says she fell in love with Cornwall on her honeymoon and went back often. But when her marriage ended, she felt she had to reclaim the place for herself.
Japan, India and Peru are some of the most memorable places on the list. What's one country or moment from your travel that you will never forget?
The Emperor stayed in a hotel in the mountains of Japan. It was a bit shabby these days. But the tour company said,  we think you'll really love it. So we got there. And we walked into this place.
"We'll have to move on more quickly.  Anyway, the next morning, when she was feeling better  and I was feeling refreshed, we went for a walk," he said. "It was a great way to start the day."
The onsen baths were set in the most beautiful Japanese garden. The baths were in a beautifully shaped garden, not far from Mount Fuji.
"I was tired and crab and bad tempered. And today, I said to myself,  go from one extreme to the other," he says.
This is a painting by Val Hudson,  who's a dear friend of mine. And kind of, I like the different textures in it. She rubs...  She puts paint on. And then she kind of takes some of the wax off.
"You get that sort of meditative feel.  And you get drawn into it by the different colors. Just the depth of color that this involves me in," she says.

I grew up in Southeast London, in a very big green area called Blacklea. It borders onto Greenwich Park,  which is also a big space of green. Blackheath Village, as it's called,  is an old village. It doesn't look like a classic

Blackheath is quite a posh place. There's quite a lot of money there, and there always has been. It was full of people who were...  There were some people who'd fled,  I think, in the war from places  where they were ostrac
You had elocution, right?  Yeah.  What was that like?  It's funny because they didn't call them  elocutions classes.  So the girls whose parents had aspirations.

"I'm actually a Cockney by birth,  because I was born within the Sound of Bobel" "I can talk like that if I wanna, right?  But my sister had a very different way of speaking to me because of our school"
"We set, we had books, I'm not kidding you,  which said things like,  the rain in Spain falls mainly on the plain.  You had to stand well  and you had to give this kind of speech"
There was an underlying sense of training a girl to talk properly. The classic line, which is in Pygmalion, is  talking about training a girls to talk proper.
"I didn't ever question it.  that they were doing, it was a good thing to be done.  On the other hand, what it leaves you with,  I don't know," he says.
"I don't sound like my family sounds. And often when you sound like this,  people think you're stuck up or a snob," he says.
"I don't know why it stuck with me.  because I've learned to talk like this.  I think it's interesting putting it on, yeah.  So of course, and of course we were taught to do that"

When I came to Sheffield,  I did stick out like a sore thumb. There is something about the way that you speak. It's not just that you have the short A's like bath and grab and pass. You also have quite a lot of ways  of saying things that

When he first came to Sheffield he had an identity crisis about his accent. It was a sense of feeling uncomfortable in a new place where he didn't belong. There were a lot of Southerners who came up  at the same time as he did.
"I think, I mean, accent,  I'm grateful for what I've had.  It has.  But I think,  accent, I'm  grateful for it," he says.

I'm from Sheffield,  born and bred in Sheffield, and I don't really speak like other people from Sheffield. So occasionally, if I go out,  or speak with a broader accent,  I will change my accent. It's difficult to know what my accent really is

I will end up sounding not broad-cockneyed,  but I will enjoy getting away. It's a part of report building, isn't it?  You kind of follow the pace of somebody. So there's quite a clipped pace in London,  quite quick speaking,
"Your accent is quite unique. Your T's at the back of your throat,  not with your tongue behind your T, bottle.  and you do the glottal stop"
"I wonder,  do people comment on it now?  Yes, a taxi driver commented on it the other day.  Do people ever comment, even now, I wonder?"
"He was a nice guy, I didn't take any offense,  and I think it was just genuine curiosity.  He said to me, you're not from around here, are you?"
"My accent's  quite unique, in a way,  what the training that I got at school  to talk in a particular way was to make you  like everybody else who spoke that way"
Alfie says his accent is unique for the area. He says there are other differences in the way that we speak.
"It might be quite difficult for some people.  Sorry, everybody. No, no, no. But it's good," he said.

As a lifelong English teacher,  I'm always listening not only to people's accents,  but also to their word choices,  their phrase, their idiosyncrasies. Some people have specific words  that only they say or they use. I just think it's absolutely fascinating.
"I'm articulate is what they say.  And I don't know where that comes from. I just have that.  I've just learned," he says.
I just love words.  And that's what I love, you know.  I've got loads of them in my pocket.  So I like to use as many of them.
Harry was born in Sheffield and has short A's, but he talks well, like you do, in inverting. So Harry, my son who's  a Yorkshireman, he's got short A’s,  but he’ll talk well.

Harry has friends who've gone to boarding school and have RP accents. Some of those friends, I can't say for certain,  but one story that he's told me is that people say to him,  your accent's really strong, Harry. And it's because of the

I think there's lots of things about class that have changed. So education's become more available  irrespective of your class. Jobs have become moreavailable. We see more representation from different classes.
People are still often interested  in your background. And voice is one of the quickest ways  that you start to get a sense of it. Yeah, it's interesting because I don't think on TV and on the radio.
"I can't change it and I never will.  I can ever say far-off.  It has to be bad"
I started saying cup rather than cook. That did change in my accent, I noticed. But yeah, accent definitely does say something. Do you like that? I think if it was free of judgment.

People assume that you're not as bright if you speak a certain way. People see that voice is another way to sort of sort.  Do you sound like me or not? People like our praise that we sort of talk like.
I don't remember feeling any pressure at school. I think I just didn't, I didn't question it. I just did the classes. I was schooled by then, that's how I spoke.

There's a way of talking light and professional, isn't there, as well as the accent that you've got. So you have to kind of tune into those things and know, you know, I worked in a theater box office. You have to give service  and work in
You don't say, yeah, you want.  You know, say, good evening, sir. How can I help you?  So interesting.  So you've got to behave in a certain way  in how you talk.

"My mom's first job was working in the market,  the vegetable market. She also had to speak in a certain way,  have good banter, and very kind of the same,  but opposite" "I'd have given her a wide berth.  I mean,

I like that in environments, isn't it? There's a certain way of vocabulary,  a dialect for different jobs, even.  You adjust your language in the same way  that you speak a different language  in a different country.
"Take your time.  So it is time for the last set,  which is the reflective and what I call challenge. So take your time," he said.
Don't feel pressured to answer right away. So the first question is,  what's one thing you learned?
What is one thing you wish more people understood?  Good question. I don't know. I just want people to be happy.
I'm a big fan of kindness, politeness, and passion. I think I'm not a very patient person. Patience, and there's something about in the NHS,  there's this word civility.

I think I've been lucky  to have had a lot of good things in my life. And I've earned reasonably well,  and been able to support myself. But I also realise the value of simplicity. Having time to really savour things and take pleasure in small things really
I don't know what the quotas are.  I'll try and find out. The next is, if you could talk to your younger self, what would you say?
"Trust your instincts more. Trust your-  Would you like to elaborate?  Yeah, I think there are times when you have a sense  that something is right for you,  and I've really acted on that"
"I wish I hadn't. I did it, that was how it was,  and I think I could have done something sooner. My instinct was telling me sooner  to wake up and do it"
If you could keep one book,  what would it be, and why? It'd probably be a Jane Austen novel. It might be Pride and Prejudice,  which is quite funny.
Carol says a good life means being healthy,  and doing things with people you love. Staying connected to your family is also important, she says.
